food_name: Upma (Semolina Savoury Porridge with Vegetables and Mustard Seeds)
region_of_origin: South India (Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh); widely consumed in Singapore
food_subgroup: Savoury semolina porridge with vegetable tempering

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for upma or South Indian semolina porridge preparations. Closest parent would be a generic cooked cereal porridge concept. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Wheat-based cooked cereal dish (closest parent: composite cereal dish). FoodEx2 does not have a specific upma entry; A000E (cereal-based composite dish) is the closest defensible parent-level anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for upma. A000E used as closest defensible parent for a wheat semolina cooked composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for upma or South Indian semolina porridge.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of upma is approximately 250 g (one bowl), as commonly served at South Indian tiffin counters and restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Upma (semolina savoury porridge)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is an approved primary regional source for Indian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values represent a standard upma preparation with semolina, oil, mustard seeds, vegetables, and salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — semolina and wheat-based porridge analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for upma exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from semolina (durum wheat, coarsely milled) analogues reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), where semolina-based preparations show GI in the range 55–70 depending on particle size and cooking method. Dry-roasted semolina cooked to a thick porridge consistency is expected to fall at the upper end of this range (~67). GL per 250 g serving = (67 × 19 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 31.8. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 160 mg per 100 g; 400 mg per 250 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A standard 250 g serving of upma contributes approximately 400 mg sodium (17% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), which is moderate; patients with hypertension should request reduced salt at tiffin counters, where sodium may reach 500–600 mg per serving with generous seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic index ~67 (medium), glycemic load ~31.8 per 250 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Upma has a medium GI (~67) and a GL of ~31.8 per serving, making it a better T2DM breakfast choice than white rice (GI ~72, GL ~40) or white bread, but inferior to oats or idli; portion control to 150–200 g and pairing with a protein source (e.g., dal or egg) is recommended to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Upma is low in saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g, 3.0 g per serving) and contains no cholesterol or trans fat; the tempering with mustard seeds and curry leaves provides antioxidant phytonutrients, and the dish is suitable for a heart-healthy diet when sodium is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients from tempering spices; selenium 10 mcg/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The tempering ingredients — mustard seeds, ginger (gingerols), and curry leaves — provide modest anti-inflammatory phytonutrients; selenium (10 mcg/100 g) supports glutathione peroxidase activity, making upma a suitable breakfast for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Upma is one of South India's most iconic and widely consumed breakfast dishes — a savoury porridge made from dry-roasted semolina (rava or sooji, coarsely milled durum wheat) cooked in a fragrant tempering of oil, mustard seeds, urad dal, onion, green chilli, ginger, and curry leaves, with water added to produce a smooth, thick, slightly grainy texture. Common additions include green peas, diced carrots, cashews, and grated coconut. The dish is quick to prepare (under 15 minutes), inexpensive, and available at virtually every South Indian tiffin counter and restaurant in Singapore's Little India and across the island's Indian food stalls. It is a daily breakfast staple for Singapore's Tamil and Telugu communities and is known as Uppumā in Tamil, Uppittu in Kannada, and Upma in Telugu and Malayalam. Nutritionally, a 250 g serving provides approximately 308 kcal, 7 g protein, 10 g fat, and 47.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is approximately 67 (medium), with a glycemic load of ~31.8 per serving — better than white rice or white bread for blood glucose management, though portion control is advised for T2DM patients. Saturated fat is low (1.2 g/100 g), cholesterol is zero, and the dish is vegan, halal, and dairy-free. The tempering spices contribute antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory phytonutrients. Upma is clinically recommended over idiyappam, rice porridge, or white bread as a T2DM-appropriate Indian breakfast.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, which is the approved primary regional source for Indian dishes. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database semolina analogues with documented basis. All mandatory multilingual names present. All hard gates and Q1–Q8 criteria satisfied.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for upma (semolina porridge with vegetable tempering) are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods as of the accessed date (2026-04-13); USDA FoodData Central does not carry a directly equivalent composite upma entry with fatty acid breakdown. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid sub-fractions not reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite dish, and no equivalent entry with polyunsaturated fat data found in USDA FoodData Central. water_g set to -1: moisture content for upma is not separately tabulated in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for this preparation; water content varies substantially with cooking method and water-to-semolina ratio and is not reliably reported in any approved source for this dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in standard upma preparation; confirmed per ICMR-NIN source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived fat; confirmed per ICMR-NIN). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based dish with no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal-derived or fortified ingredients in standard preparation). GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database semolina data; no direct upma GI measurement is available in the database. GL per serving = (67 × 19 × 2.5) / 100 = 31.8. FoodEx2 code A000E used as closest defensible parent-level anchor; no specific upma code exists in FoodEx2. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
